one_line_takeaway: A regulation-aware perspective piece that taxonomizes data-poisoning attacks (indiscriminate/targeted/backdoor across training-from-scratch/fine-tuning/outsourcing) and defenses, concluding that ML security against poisoning is still immature -- attacks don't scale, defenses are attack-specific and rarely tested against adaptive attacks.
key_finding: The central conclusion is a negative answer to the title: the field is not 'there yet.' The authors argue effective/stealthy optimization-based poisoning attacks do not scale to large modern DNNs, while efficient heuristic attacks are brittle against defenses; defenses are largely attack-specific (one defense mitigates one attack) and are rarely evaluated against adaptive attacks that know the defense, so their real-world robustness is poorly understood. They also highlight that many threat models (e.g., control over the entire training set) are unrealistic, and that poisoning interacts with other trustworthiness dimensions (privacy, fairness) in ways the community understands poorly. Privacy-enhancing gradient shaping (Hong et al.) can incidentally mitigate poisoning, illustrating cross-cutting defense effects.
core_contribution: A high-level overview/perspective article (accepted at IEEE Computer, 2024) on machine-learning security against data poisoning. Its own contribution is NOT any new attack or defense algorithm. The scenario/goal taxonomy it uses is explicitly ADOPTED from the authors' own prior in-depth technical survey ('Wild Patterns Reloaded', ACM Computing Surveys 2023, ref [2]) -- the paper states it categorizes attacks 'following the categorization presented in [2].' What is distinctive here, by the authors' own statement (they focus on this rather than re-doing [2]'s technical survey), is: (i) framing poisoning within the broader context of ML trustworthiness and AI regulation (EU AI Act, ENISA), and (ii) presenting standard security-engineering principles (Saltzer & Schroeder -- access control, monitoring, audit trails) that can be integrated with ML-specific defenses. It rounds this out by (iii) laying out open challenges and answering its titular question: no, ML security against data poisoning is 'not there yet.'
threat_model: Data poisoning: an adversary tampers with the training data (or the outsourced training process) to compromise the resulting model. The paper systematizes three attacker-knowledge/deployment scenarios: (i) training-from-scratch, (ii) fine-tuning of a pretrained model, and (iii) model-training/outsourcing (including federated learning as a special case, where even a single malicious participant can impair the shared model). Under limited knowledge, attackers exploit transferability via surrogate models/datasets (Demontis et al.). It further distinguishes three attack goals: indiscriminate (availability -> lower overall accuracy), targeted (integrity -> misclassify a specific target sample while keeping clean accuracy high), and backdoor (integrity -> misclassify any input carrying a trigger). Clean-label variants aim for stealth so poisoned samples look consistent with their labels. Outsourcing attacks must additionally survive the victim's validation phase to stay stealthy.
limitations: As an overview it is intentionally high-level and non-technical, with no experiments, benchmarks, or quantitative comparisons of its own -- it relies entirely on cited results. Scope is deliberately narrowed to poisoning of classification models; evasion and privacy attacks are only mentioned, deferring to the ENISA report. The authors themselves flag the open challenges (impractical threat models, poor attack scalability, attack-specific and under-evaluated defenses, lack of adaptive-attack evaluation, and the absence of standardized industrial-grade benchmarks) as unresolved. The article names concrete gaps rather than resolving any.
